public_goals: Keep the logistics process flexible enough to handle real-life schedule variation.
Preserve spontaneous family time without needing advance notice for every activity.
Avoid over-formalizing what should still be a functional co-parenting relationship.
private_concerns: Worries that too much structure will make the separation feel more adversarial than it needs to be.
Feels that the other parent's need for written schedules may reflect distrust rather than practicality.
Wants the process to feel collaborative and mutual rather than compliance-oriented.
red_lines: Will not accept a logistics protocol so rigid that normal life disruptions create automatic conflict.
Will not agree to a process that treats any scheduling adjustment as a violation.
emotional_triggers: the structure being framed as needed because they are unreliable
written processes feeling like surveillance or distrust
spontaneous parenting time treated as a problem to be eliminated
disclosure_tendencies: offers constructive structure once the default-guide framing is on the table rather than a rigid protocol
response_to_perceived_bias_or_pressure: becomes more collaborative when structure is framed as a mutual default rather than one-sided compliance
pushes back if process sounds like it is designed to catch them out
starting_positions: Primary position: logistics should be agreed by mutual understanding rather than a rigid written protocol.
Secondary position: if something written helps, it should be a default guide rather than a binding rule.
likely_openings: I am fine working this out, I just do not want it to feel like a contract.
We both want what is good for the child, I think we can keep this reasonable.
